channel.title: Gray Oak Journal
channel.description: Essays on veterinary leadership, mentorship, culture, and practice management by Dr. Susan Ries Valashinas, DVM.

item.title: Empathy vs. Sympathy: The Most Misused Word in Veterinary Medicine
item.description: A veterinarian on why sympathy, not empathy, is the emotion that survives a veterinary career, and what burnout research says about empathic distress.

item.title: Doctor-First Room Flow: A Faster Way to Run Veterinary Sick Appointments
item.description: A veterinarian on why seeing sick cases first, before the assistant takes the history, speeds up appointments, protects the team, and gets pets into diagnostics sooner.

item.title: Veterinary Scheduling: How to Get Everyone Out on Time
item.description: Phantom appointments and gatekeeping are symptoms of broken veterinary scheduling. Six causes, and the fix for each one, so your team gets home on time.

item.title: Accountability in Veterinary Leadership: Why It Is Not Optional
item.description: Two stories on what happens when leaders fail to hold people accountable, plus a six-step framework for getting it right. From The Gray Oak Journal.

item.title: We Teach Our Clients How to Treat Us
item.description: We teach our clients how to treat us. Sometimes intentionally, sometimes by answering texts at 10:47 p.m. A veterinarian on boundaries, systems, and the special client problem.

item.title: Could've, Should've, Would've: Veterinary Self-Doubt at 3am
item.description: A veterinarian on the 3am gremlin, the question her therapist asked, and how to tell veterinary self-doubt from actual malpractice.

item.title: Talk the Ear Off of Corn: Helping Your Veterinary Team Manage Chatty Clients
item.description: How to help your veterinary team redirect chatty clients without losing warmth. Practical phrases, coaching tools, and why protecting the schedule is an act of kindness.

item.title: When the Leader Is the Emergency: How to Lead Your Team Through Crisis and Trauma
item.description: What happens when the person your team relies on is the one who needs help. A real story about crisis leadership, debriefs, and what near-misses reveal about your culture.

item.title: The Tail on the Counter
item.description: How strong veterinary leaders teach their teams to prioritize the right work, in the right order, at the right time. Sequence, deadlines, and why the reasoning matters.

item.title: She Did Not Need to Be Managed More. She Needed to Be Trusted More.
item.description: Why leaders fail to empower employees, and what it costs them. A practical look at hovering, micromanagement, and the bottleneck you built by accident.

item.title: The Family Crest Would Be a Donkey
item.description: Stubborn does not need instructions. A veterinarian on recognizing the trait in yourself, understanding why people resist change, and knowing when patience stops being leadership.

item.title: Lost in Translation
item.description: If your client leaves the room confused, it does not matter how correct you were. Clear communication is not a soft skill. It is a clinical skill, a service skill, and a leadership responsibility.

item.title: The Lost Art of Porch Sitting
item.description: Most veterinary leaders are present but not observing. Dr. V on why stillness is a leadership skill, and what you miss when you never stop moving.

item.title: Some People Would Rather Quit Than Say Sorry
item.description: When an employee walks out rather than apologize, it reveals a personality trait that can quietly destroy your veterinary team's culture and patient safety.

item.title: "Try Again Tomorrow": Because Today Was a Disaster
item.description: Snapping doesn't make you a bad person. Refusing to reflect on it does. Self-reflection, apologies, and fixing the systems behind veterinary burnout.

item.title: Decision Fatigue Is Real. And So Is My Relationship With Reality TV
item.description: When you make high-stakes decisions all day, your brain needs real rest. A veterinarian on decision fatigue, burnout, and building a daily off switch.

item.title: Rip the Band-Aid, Not the Skin
item.description: A veterinarian with 20+ years of leadership experience on how to terminate an employee with clarity and dignity, without unnecessary damage to them, to you, or to your team.

item.title: One Bad Apple Will Ruin the Whole Orchard
item.description: A veterinary leader with 20 years of practice experience on knowing when coaching has run its course, and why waiting too long costs more than you think.

item.title: The Unicorn in Sensible Shoes
item.description: We have become so accustomed to indifferent service that when someone simply does their job well, we talk about it as though we have spotted a unicorn in sensible shoes.

item.title: The Best Veterinarian in Town Can Still Lose to a Friendlier Front Desk
item.description: Great veterinary medicine is not enough. The service experience surrounding it determines whether clients return, refer others, or quietly disappear. A leadership perspective on hiring, culture, and the mindset shift most hospitals have not made yet.
